title: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
summary: Find eigenvalues and eigenvectors of square matrices and understand their geometric meaning.
content_html:
An eigenvector v of matrix A is a non-zero vector that only changes by a scalar factor λ (the eigenvalue) when A is applied:

Av = λv

Finding Eigenvalues

Rearranging: (A − λI)v = 0. For non-zero v, we need:

det(A − λI) = 0   ← characteristic equation

Example: A = [4 1; 2 3]

det([4−λ  1; 2  3−λ]) = 0
(4−λ)(3−λ) − 2 = 0
λ² − 7λ + 10 = 0
(λ−5)(λ−2) = 0
λ₁ = 5, λ₂ = 2

Finding Eigenvectors

For each λ, solve (A − λI)v = 0:

For λ=5: [−1 1; 2 −2]v = 0 → v = (1,1)
For λ=2: [2  1; 2  1]v = 0 → v = (1,−2)

Applications

  • PCA (data science): principal components are eigenvectors
  • Stability analysis: eigenvalues determine system stability
  • Google PageRank: finds dominant eigenvector

Diagonalization

If A has n linearly independent eigenvectors: A = PDP⁻¹ where D is diagonal with eigenvalues, P is eigenvector matrix.

exercises.title: Eigenvalue definition
exercises.prompt: If Av = λv, then λ is called the:
options: Eigenvalue of A corresponding to eigenvector v
exercises.answer: Eigenvalue of A corresponding to eigenvector v

exercises.title: Geometric meaning

options: Stretches or flips v along its own direction by factor λ
exercises.answer: Stretches or flips v along its own direction by factor λ
